---
title: "AI Agents as Stochastic Replicators: Measuring Researcher-Induced Estimation Uncertainty"
author:
  - name: Brett A. McCully
    affiliations:
      - name: Collegio Carlo Alberto
filters:
  - main_text_wordcount.lua
header-includes:
  - |
    \usepackage{booktabs}
    \usepackage{graphicx}
    \usepackage{caption}
    \usepackage{subcaption}
    \usepackage[section]{placeins}
    \usepackage[dvipsnames]{xcolor}
    \input{../NHK-replications/meta_analysis_expanded/paper_macros.tex}
    \input{economics_letters_wordcount.tex}
    \newcommand{\titleaffiliation}{Collegio Carlo Alberto}
    \newcommand{\titledate}{May 19, 2026}
    \newcommand{\titlenote}{brett.mccully@carloalberto.org. I thank Yagan Hazard for helpful conversations. Any errors are my own.}
    \makeatletter
    \renewcommand{\maketitle}{
      \thispagestyle{empty}
      \begin{center}
      {\LARGE \@title\textsuperscript{*} \par}
      \vspace{1.5em}
      {\large \@author \par}
      \vspace{0.5em}
      {\normalsize \titleaffiliation \par}
      \vspace{0.75em}
      {\normalsize \titledate \par}
      \vspace{0.75em}
      {\footnotesize \textit{Word count (main text):} \MainTextWordCount \par}
      \end{center}
      \vspace{1.5em}
    }
    \makeatother
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    classoption: [12pt]
    geometry: margin=1in
    linestretch: 1.5
    cite-method: natbib
    biblio-style: ecca
bibliography: bibliography.bib
date: "May 22, 2026"
number-sections: true
number-depth: 2
abstract: |
  Reported standard errors capture sampling uncertainty conditional on one set of researcher decisions, but defensible alternatives in sample construction and specification can shift estimates substantially. I draw repeated, independent AI agents that receive the same prompt and dataset but generate different research choices. Applying the method to the immigration policy--employment question studied by \citet{huntingtonklein2025sources} using many human analysts, AI agents generate \aiAnalyticN{} estimates. The distribution of coefficients and sample size reasonably match that of the human analysts, suggesting AI agents to be useful proxies for human researchers when quantifying estimate uncertainty due to variation in researcher choices.
---

```{=latex}
\begin{quotation}
\footnotesize
\noindent \textbf{Keywords:} Researcher degrees of freedom, replication, specification uncertainty, AI agents

\medskip
\noindent \textbf{JEL Codes:} C12, C15, C18
\end{quotation}

\vfill
\noindent\rule{0.33\textwidth}{0.4pt}
\par
\noindent {\footnotesize \textsuperscript{*}\titlenote\par}

\newpage
```

# Introduction {#sec-intro}

Empirical papers routinely report standard errors that quantify sampling uncertainty. These standard errors are conditional on a set of researcher decisions: which dataset to analyze, how to define the estimation sample, how to construct outcomes and treatments, and which specification and standard-error adjustments to use. A growing body of evidence shows that researchers making different  decisions can produce meaningfully different estimates from the same underlying data[@silberzahn2018many; @huntington2021influence; @breznau2022observing]. This implies an additional source of uncertainty beyond standard errors capturing sampling variation: researcher degrees of freedom.

Measuring parameter uncertainty due to researcher degrees of freedom at scale is challenging. Multi-analyst studies are expensive, requiring extensive recruitment and coordination. Pre-analysis plans constrain discretion but do not measure the sensitivity of results to alternative, still-defensible research choices [@olken2015promises]. Specification-curve and multiverse analyses require the original analyst to enumerate and implement various alternatives [@simonsohn2020specification; @steegen2016multiverse].

I overcome these limitations using a novel approach: AI agents that perform empirical analyses at scale.\footnote{There are at least 3 other concurrent, unpublished papers that attempt to do broadly what I propose in this article: \citet{gao2026nonstandard}, \citet{grundl2026comparison}, and \citet{huang2026aierrors}. Given the timing of the release of these working papers and the work on my own public GitHub repository for this article dating back to January 2026 (see \url{https://github.com/brettmcc/LLM-bootstrapping}), this is a clear case of parallel invention.} Each agent receives the same natural-language research prompt and dataset but independently generates a complete empirical specification---sample restrictions, outcome and treatment definitions, controls, fixed effects, and standard-error adjustments. The inherent stochasticity of the large language models which power the AI agents ensures each agent makes different research choices.

I apply this approach to the question studied by many analysts in @huntingtonklein2025sources: how does eligibility for the Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals program affect the probability of full-time employment?
Across \aiAnalyticN{} agent-generated specifications, I obtain an interquartile range from \aiIqrLower{} to \aiIqrUpper{}, strongly resembling the human many-analyst coefficient distribution.

This paper makes two main contributions. First, I provide an open-source, scalable approach to multi-analyst designs that lets researchers quantify researcher-induced uncertainty. Second, I show that repeated AI-agent runs provide a computationally tractable way to approximate the distribution of human research choices at scale: each run generates an interpretable, machine-readable specification that can be executed without human intervention.

Multi-analyst studies document how research choices affect results but are costly to run at scale [@silberzahn2018many; @breznau2022observing; @brownlees2024nonstandard]. Specification-curve and multiverse analyses automate the enumeration of alternatives but require the original analyst to define the choice set [@simonsohn2020specification; @steegen2016multiverse]. I introduce AI-agent-enumerated multiverses, in which the agents themselves define the choice set. Recent work uses LLMs as simulated economic agents [@horton2023large] and research tools [@korinek2023generative; @novymarx2026ai]; this paper applies them to measuring research-choice variation. @sec-method describes the agent pipeline and the DACA application; @sec-results reports the estimate distribution, specification heterogeneity, and the comparison to the benchmark.

# Method {#sec-method}

## AI agents as empirical researchers
For a given empirical research question, social scientists have substantial degrees of freedom in shaping the analysis. Such choices include selecting which observations make it into their analysis sample, the estimation method used, which control variables to include, how to cluster standard errors, and so on. Variation across researcher choices may reflect differences in training and pre-existing biases.

For example, in the empirical application below I consider the effect of the immigration policy, Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals (DACA), on the likelihood of full-time work among affected immigrants. Researchers may make different defensible choices about age bounds, full-time work definitions, eligibility proxies, and whether to estimate a linear or nonlinear model. Different combinations of these choices imply different research designs.

AI agents, such as ChatGPT or Claude Code, provide inherently take different approaches to a given research prompt. By construction, AI responses are non-deterministic: the same prompt can generate a range of responses. By submitting a prompt to estimate a given empirical parameter many times to an AI agent, I propose to simulate at much lower cost a many-analyst approach for generating bounds on the degree of researcher-induced parameter estimation uncertainty.\footnote{Other researchers have adopted the catchier  term 'non-standard' errors to describe the bounds resulting from this type of exercise [@brownlees2024nonstandard].}

Differently from the trained human researchers involved in previous multi-analyst studies, AI agents may take research choices far outside disciplinary norms and may make a set of choices which are less internally coherent. The degree to which AI agents hew to human patterns of research choices is therefore an empirical question. This paper replicates an existing human multianalyst study by @nickchk2022manyeconomists and compares the distribution of research choices and the resulting estimates.

Ex-ante, why might AI agents produce plausible research choices? Their underlying models are trained on academic articles, codebases, and empirical templates [@gao2020pile, @chen2021codex]---the same materials from which researchers learn to define samples, write regression equations, and choose standard-error adjustments. AI agent outputs can therefore be interpreted as draws from a compressed, noisy representation of standard empirical social science practice.

## Application

I apply the pipeline to the question answered by many analysts in @huntingtonklein2025sources. The authors recruit nearly 200 economists and ask each to independently answer the same causal question using the same instructions and raw data: how did eligibility for the Defferred Action for Childhood Arrivals immigration policy affect the probability of full-time employment among eligible immigrants? Participants are given a data codebook and the research question but otherwise exercise broad discretion over sample construction, variable definitions, and specification choices. In this study I replicate the initial task from @huntingtonklein2025sources in which participants had maximum flexibility to make research choices.

The computing pipeline works as follows. An AI agent is provided with the raw materials to answer the research question: an IPUMS extract and codebook with all variables used by human analysts in @nickchk2022manyeconomists (derived from their public repository), a supplementary state policy dataset and codebook, and instructions adapted from those given to human analysts.\footnote{All material passed to AI agents are available at https://github.com/brettmcc/LLM-bootstrapping/tree/master/NHK-replications/replication-materials.} Each AI agent is isolated to their own run directory, so they cannot learn from previous agent runs. The agent then writes a JSON file outlining their model specification, outcome definition, sample selection, and treatment definition.\footnote{For example, one retained specification estimates \texttt{full\_time} on \texttt{treated\_post}, treatment and post indicators, age terms, sex indicators, state fixed effects, and year fixed effects; defines full-time work as usual hours worked greater than or equal to 35; restricts the sample by Hispanic origin, Mexican birthplace, non-citizenship, pre-2008 immigration, childhood arrival, birth cohort, group-quarters status, year, and working age; and defines treatment using the birth-year eligibility proxy.} The agent then writes a Python script to implement their estimation and reports the resulting sample size and preferred coefficient.

I use two frontier AI models---GPT-5.4-mini (OpenAI) and Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic)\footnote{I exclude \aiExcludedModelRuns{} exploratory runs with Claude's smaller and less advanced Haiku 4.5 model from the analysis sample. That cohort yielded one run with standard error of 0 and two more with extremely high coefficient estimates. Researchers applying my approach should therefore take heed that smaller models with insufficient reasoning may not be suitable for this type of task.}---accessed through GitHub Copilot CLI. Each run takes 2--10 minutes and has minimal cost when using a subscription,\footnote{It's worth noting the amount of inference provided by Github Copilot is set to fall dramatically at the beginning of June, so researchers would likely do better to leverage Claude Code or OpenAI's Codex in the future.} making large-scale replication affordable for individual researchers.

# Results {#sec-results}

## Distribution of estimates



Figure~\ref{fig:comparison-boxplots} compares the box-and-whisker summaries for the retained AI analyses and the original NHK human Task 1 submissions. I compare these patterns to the benchmark on three dimensions. First, central tendency is similar: the AI median (\aiMedianEffect{}) is close to the Task 1 median (\benchmarkMedianEffect{}) and both means are small and positive (\aiMeanEffect{} versus \benchmarkMeanEffect{}). Second, both distributions are wide relative to individual standard errors, confirming that specification choices, not sampling noise, drive the spread. Third, the AI distribution is notably wider (IQR width \aiIqrWidth{} versus \benchmarkIqrWidth{}) and extends further into negative territory. This wider spread likely reflects the AI agents' broader exploration of the specification space---for instance, fewer constraints on age bounds and eligibility definitions---and the fact that different specifications can target different estimands, inflating cross-specification dispersion. I characterize the resemblance as qualitative because the input specifications differ systematically (Table~\ref{tab:methods}) and no formal distributional test is attempted.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../NHK-replications/meta_analysis_expanded/figure4_comparison_boxplots.png}
\caption{AI-agent and NHK human Task~1 box-and-whisker comparisons}
\caption*{\footnotesize \textbf{Notes:} Panels compare point-estimate distributions, point-estimate distributions weighted by inverse standard error, standard errors, and $\log$ sample sizes. The standard-error panel uses a log x-axis; the published human minimum is reported as 0.000 and is displayed at 0.0005, the three-decimal rounding boundary. AI boxes use the retained analytic sample. Human boxes use the published Task~1 summary statistics in Table~3 of \citet{huntingtonklein2025sources}. Boxes show the interquartile range, center lines show medians, and whiskers show published or sample minimum and maximum values. Grid lines are omitted to keep the box-and-whisker summaries visually dominant.}
\label{fig:comparison-boxplots}
\end{figure}
\FloatBarrier
```

Figure~\ref{fig:comparison-boxplots} shows that the AI and human distributions overlap substantially, but the AI box extends farther into negative estimates. The sample-size panel reinforces that part of the induced uncertainty comes from sample construction rather than only from estimator choice: both humans and AI agents produce large variation in effective sample sizes from the same underlying research question.

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{../NHK-replications/meta_analysis_expanded/figure3_specification_curve.png}
\caption{Specification curve for AI-agent-generated estimates}
\caption*{\footnotesize \textbf{Notes:} Estimates are sorted by point estimate. Vertical lines are 95\% confidence intervals computed from each agent-generated standard error. The figure excludes estimates outside the displayed range for visual readability; Table~\ref{tab:summary} reports the full retained distribution.}
\label{fig:specification-curve}
\end{figure}
\FloatBarrier
```

Figure~\ref{fig:specification-curve} shows that the retained AI specifications span negative, near-zero, and positive estimates even before any analyst selects among them. The curve is therefore a diagnostic for researcher-induced uncertainty: a conventional single-specification standard error would describe uncertainty around one point on this curve, not uncertainty over the curve itself.

## Specification heterogeneity

```{=latex}
\input{../NHK-replications/meta_analysis_expanded/paper_table2_methods.tex}
```

Table~\ref{tab:methods} shows that AI agents favor linear models (\aiLinearModelShare{}\% of retained runs) and the ACS person weight (\aiPerwtShare{}\%). State-clustered standard errors are the modal choice (\aiClusterStateShare{}\%). The benchmark human sample is more methodologically diverse: 82\% use linear regression, 22\% use no SE adjustment, and 25\% use sample weights. The AI agents thus converge on a narrower set of input choices---more weighting and more clustering---yet produce a wider distribution of output estimates, suggesting that sample-definition and variable-construction choices drive more dispersion than estimator choice alone.

Routine specification choices covary with estimates. Specifications with state fixed effects average \aiStateFeMean{} versus \aiNoStateFeMean{} without; those with year fixed effects average \aiYearFeMean{} versus \aiNoYearFeMean{} without. These are descriptive comparisons that conflate fixed-effects inclusion with other correlated choices, but they are directionally consistent with the benchmark finding that modeling choices materially affect point estimates.

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[!htbp]
\centering
\caption{Mean effects by control functional form: AI agents and NHK humans}
\label{tab:controls}
\small
\resizebox{\textwidth}{!}{\input{../NHK-replications/meta_analysis_expanded/table5_control_effects.tex}}
\caption*{\footnotesize \textbf{Notes:} Rows follow the construction of Table~5 in \citet{huntingtonklein2025sources}: each row is an overlapping indicator for specifications including the listed functional form, so counts do not sum to the number of estimates. AI-agent rows classify retained generated specifications using their recorded controls and fixed effects. Human columns reproduce the NHK Task~1 Table~5 counts, mean effects, standard deviations of reported effects, and mean reported standard errors.}
\end{table}
\FloatBarrier
```

Table~\ref{tab:controls} shows that control choices are another channel through which otherwise routine decisions move estimates. As in the human benchmark, rows are descriptive comparisons across overlapping specification features rather than causal effects of adding one control. AI agents use year and state fixed effects much more frequently than the NHK humans, while humans use education controls far more often. Mean effects for state/year specifications are nonetheless broadly similar across the two samples.

# Conclusion {#sec-conclusion}

The dispersion across agent-generated specifications---spanning negative to meaningfully positive effects, with wide variation in sample sizes and modeling choices---mirrors the pattern a large team of human analysts produces. Temperature-driven variation in AI research choices appears to capture a similar space of defensible decisions, offering a scalable complement to costly multi-analyst exercises.

Four limitations bear emphasis. First, the @huntingtonklein2025sources benchmark materials---including codebooks, instructions, and human submissions---were public on OSF and GitHub before these runs, and the AI models are trained on large public web corpora [@brown2020gpt3; @chen2021codex]. The prompt used here was written independently of the benchmark's prepared-data documentation and asks the agent to work from a raw ACS extract rather than the benchmark's pre-processed dataset, but I cannot rule out that the models memorized human specifications from their training corpora. Validation on a question absent from public corpora is needed. Second, different specifications can target different estimands, so the distribution mixes variation in estimation strategies with variation in targeted causal parameters. Third, model choice materially affects the induced distribution, as illustrated by the excluded Haiku exploratory cohort. Fourth, execution selection could matter in future applications if more unusual specifications are disproportionately likely to fail, even though all \aiAttemptedRuns{} runs in the retained two-model cohort produced recoverable specifications and valid numeric outputs.

For empirical practice, the pipeline offers a diagnostic: researchers can run it on their own question and data to assess sensitivity to defensible alternatives. Applying the method to other multi-analyst benchmarks---such as the immigration-attitudes study of @breznau2022observing---would test whether the AI-agent distribution generalizes beyond the DACA context.

<!-- References are generated automatically by Quarto/natbib -->
